title: ACP execute_local_tool bypasses PreToolUse hooks and rules engine
description:
## Severity
Critical
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/acp.rs:1281-1318 (execute_tool_via_acp branches + execute_local_tool)
## Violation
`execute_local_tool` invokes `tools::execute_tool_with_memory` directly — passing the permission manager but never the hook engine. The field `hook_engine` is declared `#[allow(dead_code)]` at line 119 and never consulted:

```rust
#[allow(dead_code)]
hook_engine: HookEngine,
...
let result = crate::tools::execute_tool_with_memory(&tc, None, Some(&self.permission_mgr));
```

Likewise `rules_engine` is `#[allow(dead_code)]` (line 122) and never queried before a tool call. The whole agent loop also calls `prompt::build_system_prompt(None, None, None)` (line 789), dropping both hook-injected instructions and rules context.

Result: in the ACP code path, the configured `PreToolUse` / `PostToolUse` hooks, the rules engine context injection, and any `.openclaudia/rules` content are bypassed entirely. The same project run through the proxy enforces these gates; through ACP it does not. That is a silent security/policy bypass.
## Mandated refactor
Wire `hook_engine.evaluate_pre_tool_use(...)` before every dispatch in `execute_tool_via_acp` and `execute_tool_via_openclaudia`, abort on `allowed = false`, and run the post-tool path after each result. Inject the rules-engine output and merged hook system messages into the prompt builder (replace the `build_system_prompt(None, None, None)` call with `build_system_prompt_blocks(..., Some(hook_instructions), ...)`). Delete the `#[allow(dead_code)]` once they're live so the next dead-code regression fails CI.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
